parts.answer: The only correct answer is C (it is toxic at low concentrations) A is incorrect because CO does not form an acid in the atmosphere B is incorrect because CO does not strongly absorb infrared radiation D is incorrect because CO is not the main cause of ozone layer depletion Number Correct Answer Mark A is incorrect because initiation would not have a free radical reactant and heterolytic bond breaking would form ions B is incorrect because initiation would not have a free radical reactant C is incorrect because heterolytic bond breaking would form ions Number Correct Answer Mark A is incorrect because it is the volume of butane that has not reacted B is incorrect because it is the total volume of reactants C is incorrect because it is the total volume of products Number Correct Answer Mark B is incorrect because the energy of ultraviolet radiation is insufficient to break a C−H bond C is incorrect because the energy of ultraviolet radiation is insufficient to break a C−C bond D is incorrect because the energy of ultraviolet radiation is insufficient to break a C−H bond Number Correct Answer Mark H + Br → C H Br + HBr) A is incorrect because hydrogen is not formed in the reaction C is incorrect because the carbon chain does not break in the reaction D is incorrect because the carbon chain does not break in the reaction and a C=C double bond does not form Number Correct Answer Mark 8 The only correct answer is D (CH Cl ) A is incorrect because C H is non-polar B is incorrect because although CCl has polar bonds it does not have a dipole moment C is incorrect because although BCl has polar bonds it does not have a dipole moment Number Correct Answer Mark 9 The only correct answer is D (54.2) A is incorrect because the volume has been divided by the density rather than multiplied B is incorrect because the volume has been divided by the density, then divided by the atomic number C is incorrect because the volume has been divided by the relative atomic mass Number Correct

• (1) • (1) • (1) • or (1) Penalise use of displayed or structural formulae once only If displayed / semi-displayed formulae used ignore connectivity of CH groups Ignore names even if incorrect Question Number Acceptable Answers Additional Guidance Mark • dipole on hydrogen bromide molecule and structure of final product (1) • curly arrow from C=C to H and curly arrow from H-Br bond to, or just beyond, Br (1) • carbocation intermediate (1) • lone pair on Br− and curly arrow from lone pair to positive charge (1) Allow any combination of displayed, structural or skeletal formulae Allow + on bracket around the structure Do not award δ+ on intermediate Do not award δ– on Br in M4 Penalise use of half-arrows once only If minor product is shown then do not award M1 NOTE – incorrect starting alkene can score M2 and M4 only Question Number Acceptable Answers Additional Guidance Mark • expression for calculation of moles of C and H (1) • deduction of empirical formula (1) Example of calculation Element C H Expression to calculate moles 92.3 / 12 7.7 / 1 Moles (mol) =7.7 (mol) = 7.7 (mol) Ratio 1 1 Empirical formula = CH / HC No TE from M1 to M2 Question Number Acceptable Answers Additional Guidance Mark • the tertiary carbocation is (more) stable (than the primary carbocation) Accept the 3° carbocation is more stable (than the 1° carbocation) Allow a description of a 3° carbocation e.g. ‘3 methyl groups attached to the positive C’ Ignore explanations of stability even if incorrect / Ignore references to Markovnikov’s law Number Acceptable Answers Additional Guidance Mark • conversion of volume to m (1) • conversion of temperature to K and pressure to Pa (1) • rearrangement of ideal gas equation and calculation of n (1) • calculation of molar mass (1) Example of calculation 98 x 10 –6 (m ) – if V in dm then pressure must be in kPa 358 (K) and 104 000 (Pa) n = pV ÷ RT = (104 000 x 98 x 10 –6 ) ÷ (8.31 x 358) = 3.4259 x 10 –3 (mol) Molar mass = (0.267) ÷ (3.4259 x 10 –3 ) = 77.

• numerator of weighted mean expression (1) • calculation of relative atomic mass (1) Example of calculation (70 × 20.6) + (72 × 27.4) + (73 × 7.7) + (74 × 36.7) + (76 × 7.6) (70 × 20.6) + (72 × 27.4) + (73 × 7.7) + (74 × 36.7) + (76 × 7.6) = 72.703 / 72.70 / 72.7 TE on one transcription error Final answer must be to at least 1 decimal place Correct answer with no working scores 2 Ignore units Number Acceptable Answers Additional Guidance Mark

• 1s 2s 2p 3s 3p 3d 4s 4p Allow [Ar] 3d 4s 4p Allow 4s immediately before 3d Allow 3p x 3p y 3p z Allow numbers of electrons not shown as superscripts Allow upper case letters for ‘S’, ‘P’ and ‘D’ ‘ Number Acceptable Answers Additional Guidance Mark • calculation of ∑M r (all reactants) / ∑M r (all products) (1) • calculation of atom economy as a percentage (1) Example of calculation 46+72.6 +48+23+10.8+4+18 = 222.4 / 72.6+4+80+23+10.8+32) = 222.4 (76.6 ÷ 222.4) × 100 = 34.442% Allow TE in M2 from M1 provided atom economy is less than 100% Allow use of A r = 72.7 for Ge calculated in (b) which gives the answer 34.472% Ignore SF except 1 SF Number Acceptable Answers Additional Guidance Mark • 3 bond pairs between Ge atom and three H atoms (1) • lone pair on Ge atom (and charge on ion) (1) Allow any combination of dots and / or crosses Ignore missing charge on ion Ignore lines showing covalent bonds Number Acceptable Answers Additional Guidance Mark • trigonal pyramid(al) (1) • angle between 106 and 108° (1) Allow pyramidal Comment – the correct shape and angle are standalone marks. However allow trigonal planar and 120° for 2 marks if (d)(ii) shows only 3 bond pairs. Number Acceptable Answers Additional Guidance Mark • calculation of maximum mass of germane in laboratory in mg (1) • conversion of mass from mg to g (1) • calculation of moles of germane (1) • calculation of maximum number of germane molecules in laboratory (1) NOTE – do not award M1 and M2 if there is evidence that candidates believe the values 147.84 / 0.14784 are moles. Such answers are likely to only be able to access M4 Example of calculation 0.640 × 231 = 147.84 (mg) 147.84 ÷ 1000 = 0.14784 (g) Allow TE from M1 to M2 = 0.14784 ÷ 76.6 = 1.9300 × 10 –3 (mol) Allow TE from M2 to M3 1.9300 × 10 –3 × 6.02 × 10 = 1.1619 × 10 (molecules) Allow TE from M3 to M4 Allow use of 76.7 for 76.6 giving 1.1604 × 10 Correct answer with no working scores 4 Ignore SF except 1 SF Question Number Acceptable Answers Add

Allow skeletal, structural or displayed or any combination Ignore ‘n’ before or after structure Ignore connectivity of CH and CH groups Question Number Acceptable Answers Additional Guidance Mark • oxidation Allow electrophilic addition 1 Question Number Acceptable Answers Additional Guidance Mark points: • (help develop) biodegradable polymers / polymers from plant material / (polymers that can be) re-used / (polymers that can be easily) recycled (1) • remove (toxic) gases produced by incineration (of polymers) (1) • (develop processes to) convert polymers back into feedstock (for use in chemical industry) (1) • use of IR (spectroscopy) to separate polymers (for recycling) (1) Allow the term ‘plastic’ for polymers Ignore biopolymers Ignore reduce use of polymers Allow monomers / smaller molecules / more reactive molecules / more useful molecules for ‘feedstock’ Acceptable Answers Additional Guidance Mark advantage of liquid CO • non-flammable / non-toxic / readily available / CO can be reused (1) disadvantage of liquid CO • energy needed to generate (high) pressure / (high) pressure is expensive / risk of explosion under pressure / (1) Allow reverse arguments for hexane Allow can be separated (from limonene) easily Allow hexane is non-renewable / finite Ignore ‘removes CO from atmosphere’ Allow energy needed to maintain pressure Allow strength of vessel needed to withstand (high) pressure Ignore references to temperature Ignore just ‘expensive’ Do not award greenhouse gas / global warming Question Number Acceptable Answers Additional Guidance Mark • calculation of mass of limonene required in 30 cm of cleaning product (1) • calculation of mass of orange peel needed to produce 1 g of limonene (1) • calculation of mass of orange peel needed to produce enough limonene to make 30 cm of cleaning product, in kg (1) Example of calculation 0.841 × 30 = 25.23 (g) Ignore units in M1 1 g limonene needs (100 ÷ 1.63) g of peel = 61.3497 (g) (100 ÷ 1.63) × 25.23 = 1547.9 (g) = 1
